title: Batbout

overview.description: A soft Moroccan stovetop flatbread with a pocket similar to pita. Perfect for tagines, soups and sandwiches.

method: Combine the flour, semolina, salt and yeast in a large bowl.
Add the warm water and olive oil.
Mix until a shaggy dough forms.
=== Knead ===
Knead for 8–10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
=== First Rise ===
Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl.
Cover and leave to rise for around 1 hour, or until doubled in size.
=== Shape ===
Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces.
Shape into balls.
Flatten each ball into a disc approximately 1cm thick.
Cover with a tea towel and leave to rest for 20 minutes.
=== Cook ===
Heat a dry frying pan or cast iron skillet over a medium heat.
Cook each batbout for 3–4 minutes on each side.
Turn several times during cooking until evenly browned and puffed.
=== Cool ===
Transfer to a wire rack and allow to cool slightly before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Batbout is unusual because it is cooked on a hot pan rather than baked in an oven, giving it a soft texture and characteristic pocket.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make an enriched flatbread, develop gluten through kneading and cook bread successfully on the hob.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: One of the most versatile breads in the cookbook. It works equally well with Moroccan meals, soups and sandwiches, while requiring no oven.
chapman-tips: Don't let the frying pan become too hot or the outside will brown before the inside cooks.
Turning the bread several times helps create an even pocket.
Keep the cooked batbout covered with a clean tea towel to retain their softness.
They freeze exceptionally well and reheat in just a few minutes.
notes.leftovers: Split and toast for sandwiches or warm gently in a dry frying pan.
